diet: Increase iron-rich foods such as spinach, lentils, red meat, and fortified cereals
Include vitamin C rich foods (citrus fruits, tomatoes, berries) to improve iron absorption
Limit tea and coffee consumption as they inhibit iron absorption
Consider lean meat, poultry, and fish for better iron bioavailability
exercise: Avoid high-intensity workouts until hemoglobin improves
Prefer light walking (20-30 minutes daily) or gentle yoga
Rest adequately between exercise sessions
Gradually increase intensity as energy levels improve
Anemia Indicator.follow_up: Consult physician for complete iron studies (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Recheck hemoglobin after 6-8 weeks.

diet: Choose low glycemic index foods
Include high-fiber foods: whole grains, vegetables, legumes
Reduce simple carbohydrates and sugary beverages
Eat balanced meals with protein, fat, and carbohydrates
Practice portion control and regular meal timing
Limit fruit juice; prefer whole fruits
exercise: 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly
Add resistance training 2-3 times per week
Exercise after meals for better glucose management
Avoid prolonged sitting; take movement breaks every hour
Consistency is more important than intensity
Diabetes Risk.follow_up: HbA1c recheck in 3 months. Fasting and post-meal glucose monitoring. Early intervention prevents diabetes progression. Consult endocrinologist for comprehensive diabetes risk assessment.

diet: Reduce saturated fat intake (limit to <7% of daily calories)
Increase soluble fiber-rich foods like oats, barley, beans, and apples
Include plant sterols from nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils
Choose lean proteins and reduce red meat consumption
Avoid trans fats found in processed foods and fried items
exercise: At least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity weekly
Include brisk walking, jogging, cycling, or swimming
Add resistance training 2 days per week
Avoid sedentary behavior; take regular movement breaks
High Cholesterol.follow_up: Lipid panel recheck in 3 months. Continue monitoring LDL, HDL, and triglycerides.

diet: Ensure adequate dietary fat intake from healthy sources
Include omega-3 rich foods: fatty fish (salmon, mackerel), flax seeds, walnuts
Consume nutrient-dense whole foods with healthy fats
Avoid excessive refined carbohydrate intake
Consult nutritionist to rule out malabsorption issues
exercise: Moderate exercise with adequate caloric intake
Include strength training to maintain muscle mass
Avoid excessive cardio without proper nutrition support
Focus on overall fitness rather than weight loss
Low Cholesterol.follow_up: Investigate underlying causes (malabsorption, hyperthyroidism, malnutrition). Lipid panel recheck in 3 months.

diet: Ensure adequate iodine intake from iodized salt, seafood, and dairy
Include selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts, fish, eggs
Consume zinc from meat, legumes, seeds, and nuts
Avoid goitrogenic foods (raw cruciferous vegetables) in excess, cook them well
Maintain consistent meal timing for medication absorption
exercise: Regular moderate-intensity exercise (30-45 minutes, 3-4 times weekly)
Avoid excessive intense exercise which can stress the system
Gentle yoga or swimming are ideal options
Monitor energy levels and adjust intensity accordingly
Thyroid Disorder.follow_up: TSH level recheck in 6-8 weeks after medication adjustment. Annual thyroid function tests recommended.

diet: Reduce refined carbohydrates and added sugars
Increase whole grains, vegetables, and legumes
Limit sodium intake to <2300 mg per day
Include lean proteins and healthy fats
Maintain consistent meal portions and timing
Reduce processed food consumption
exercise: At least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity weekly
Add 2-3 sessions of resistance training per week
Include daily activity breaks and movement
Progressive increase in exercise duration and intensity
Weight management target: 5-10% reduction if overweight
Metabolic Syndrome Indicator.follow_up: Metabolic panel, fasting glucose, and blood pressure monitoring every 3 months. Consultation with endocrinologist recommended.

diet: Choose low glycemic index foods
Include high-fiber foods: whole grains, vegetables, legumes
Reduce simple carbohydrates and sugary beverages
Eat balanced meals with protein, fat, and carbohydrates
Practice portion control and regular meal timing
Limit fruit juice; prefer whole fruits
exercise: 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity weekly
Add resistance training 2-3 times per week
Exercise after meals for better glucose management
Avoid prolonged sitting; take movement breaks every hour
Consistency is more important than intensity
Elevated Blood Sugar.follow_up: HbA1c recheck in 3 months. Fasting and post-meal glucose monitoring. Early intervention prevents diabetes progression.

diet: Increase salt intake moderately (unless contraindicated)
Stay well-hydrated; drink at least 8-10 glasses of water daily
Include iron-rich foods to prevent anemia-related hypotension
Eat frequent small meals to maintain stable blood sugar
Avoid skipping meals, especially breakfast
exercise: Avoid sudden position changes and prolonged standing
Gradual increase in physical activity intensity
Compression stockings may help during exercise
Ensure adequate hydration during and after exercise
Consult physician before strenuous exercise
Low Blood Pressure.follow_up: Blood pressure monitoring in different positions (lying, sitting, standing). Investigate underlying causes if severe.

diet: Follow DASH diet: fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins
Limit sodium to <2300 mg daily (ideally <1500 mg)
Reduce processed foods and cured meats
Include potassium-rich foods: bananas, spinach, sweet potato
Limit alcohol consumption
Reduce caffeine intake
exercise: 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity weekly
Include resistance training 2 days per week
Avoid isometric exercises (holding heavy weights)
Regular physical activity can reduce BP by 5-8 mmHg
Consistency is crucial; make it a daily habit
High Blood Pressure.follow_up: Blood pressure monitoring at home daily. Medication adjustment may be needed. Recheck after 4-8 weeks of lifestyle changes.

diet: Reduce alcohol consumption or abstain completely
Avoid fatty foods; choose lean proteins and grains
Include antioxidant-rich foods: berries, leafy greens, nuts
Limit processed foods and added sugars
Stay hydrated with water and herbal teas
Consider Mediterranean or plant-based diet patterns
exercise: Regular aerobic exercise to improve liver function
Weight management if overweight (10% reduction beneficial)
Moderate-intensity exercise 30-45 minutes, 5 days weekly
Avoid strenuous exercise during acute episodes
Consistency over intensity
Elevated Liver Enzymes.follow_up: Liver function tests recheck in 4-6 weeks. Investigate causes: hepatitis, fatty liver disease, medication side effects, or alcohol use.

diet: Reduce protein intake (consult nephrologist for targets)
Limit sodium to <2000 mg daily
Monitor potassium intake based on GFR levels
Limit phosphorus from processed foods
Stay appropriately hydrated; follow physician's recommendations
Choose plant-based proteins when possible
exercise: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise 3-4 days weekly
Avoid overexertion that could stress kidneys
Stay well-hydrated during exercise
Resistance training with lighter weights and higher reps
Regular monitoring of blood pressure and kidney function
Elevated Kidney Parameters.follow_up: Renal panel (creatinine, BUN, GFR) recheck in 4-6 weeks. Nephrology referral recommended. Prevent progression to chronic kidney disease.
